core_claim: Google's Colab CLI lets AI agents run arbitrary Python on remote GPUs and TPUs under your Google identity, so the data-leakage risk is agent code-exec with your credentials and a two-way data path you have to scope down.

caption:
Google shipped a Colab CLI that runs Python on remote GPUs and TPUs straight from the terminal, and it is explicitly built so AI agents can drive it, not just people. It is open source under Apache-2.0, and it authenticates with your Google account.

That is the exposure. An agent with terminal access now has a one-command path to spin up a GPU or TPU box and run arbitrary code on Google's network, authenticated as you. If that agent gets prompt-injected, it acts with your identity. Data moves both ways through upload, download, and a mounted Drive, which is a clean exfil channel. The session token sits on your machine, so a leaked token or an over-broad scope widens the blast radius into your cloud project. The auto-teardown that deletes the runtime also deletes most of the forensic trail.

Treat agent code-exec as untrusted. Do not hand an agent a long-lived, broadly scoped token from your primary account. Give it a dedicated low-privilege Google account with the narrowest scope and short-lived auth, then keep the session logs the CLI can export as your audit trail.

Agent code-exec is not a convenience. It is your identity running untrusted code on someone else's GPU.

Would you let an agent run code on remote GPUs with your Google login?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Would you let an agent run code on remote GPUs with your own Google login, or only a throwaway account?

Slide 3 (risk): two open suitcases facing each other with a stream of glowing papers flowing both directions between them.
Slide 4 (mechanism): a single brass key plugged into a small lockbox that feeds a long glowing cable off into the dark.
Slide 5 (failure_point): a wide-open vault door with a key left in the lock while a small warning tag lies ignored on the floor.
Slide 6 (defense): a small modest key on a short tether beside a closed ledger book in the dark.
Slide 7 (takeaway): a single glowing fingerprint pressed onto a cold metal surface in deep shadow.
Slide 8 (cta): a single unplugged cable coiled in empty dark space, with the comment question and handle.

sources.source: Google Developers Blog: Introducing the Google Colab CLI
sources.supports: Google released a Colab CLI on June 5, 2026 that runs Python on remote GPUs and TPUs from the terminal and is designed for AI agents to drive.
OAuth2 Google-account auth by default, Apache-2.0 license, Linux and macOS support, commands including exec, run, upload, download, and drivemount.
Developers and AI agents can run Python on remote Colab GPUs and TPUs from the terminal.

narration.text: Google's new Colab CLI lets an AI agent run Python on remote GPUs and TPUs, straight from your terminal.
It authenticates with your Google account, and it is built so agents can drive it, not just people.
So don't hand an agent your main login. Give it a dedicated low-privilege account with a narrow, short-lived scope, and keep the session logs.
Agent code-exec is not a convenience. It is your identity running untrusted code on someone else's GPU.
Would you let an agent run code on remote GPUs with your Google login? Tell me below.
